home.title: English — Revision
home.intro: Reference materials for G9 IB MYP English: text types for Criterion C, literary devices, grammar essentials, reading skills, and essay practice.

macbethPage.title: Macbeth — Scene-by-Scene Analysis
macbethPage.intro: Your EOY exam will include an extract from Macbeth. The teacher's revision booklet asks you to think about these guiding questions for each scene — they unlock the highest analysis marks.
macbethPage.notice: These questions and themes come directly from your G9 English revision booklet.

themes.title: Major themes to track
items: Ambition — Macbeth's tragic flaw. 'Vaulting ambition which o'erleaps itself.'
Fate vs free will — are the witches showing the future or causing it?
Appearance vs reality — 'Fair is foul, foul is fair'; 'Look like the innocent flower, but be the serpent under't.'
Guilt — blood as motif; Lady Macbeth's sleepwalking ('Out, damned spot').
Power — corrupting, isolating; tyranny vs rightful kingship.
Gender — Lady Macbeth's 'unsex me here'; performative masculinity.
Supernatural — witches, ghosts, hallucinations as both real and psychological.
Order vs chaos — regicide breaks the Great Chain of Being; nature reflects political disorder.

act1.title: Act I — Temptation and decision
scenes: Scene 1 (Witches' opening): How does the chant set the tone? How does 'fair is foul' introduce themes? What does this short scene suggest about supernatural influence?
Scene 2 (Battle report): What qualities of Macbeth come through in the Captain's description? How does violent imagery shape early impressions? What might his celebrated violence foreshadow?
Scene 3 (Witches meet Macbeth): How does Macbeth react to the prophecy? Contrast Banquo's caution with Macbeth's curiosity. How does this scene introduce temptation?
Scene 4 (Duncan names Malcolm heir): Why is Macbeth's aside about 'black and deep desires' significant? How does it contrast appearance and reality?
Scene 5 (Lady Macbeth's soliloquy): What does she reveal about her ambitions and values? How does Shakespeare challenge traditional gender roles? Is she more ruthless than Macbeth here?
Scene 6 (Duncan arrives): Why is Duncan's description of the castle ironic? How does this scene explore deception?

Scene 7 (Macbeth's soliloquy and Lady Macbeth's persuasion): What reasons does Macbeth give for not killing Duncan? How does Lady Macbeth manipulate him? How responsible is Macbeth for his own choice?

act2.title: Act II — The murder
scenes: Scene 1 (Dagger soliloquy): What does the vision suggest about Macbeth's mental state? How does hallucination imagery create tension?
Scene 2 (After the murder): How do Macbeth's and Lady Macbeth's reactions differ? What is revealed through the blood and guilt motif?
Scene 3 (Porter scene + discovery): How does the Porter's comedy ironically heighten horror? How does Shakespeare suggest chaos now governs Scotland?
Scene 4 (Unnatural events): What do strange events in nature reflect about human affairs? How is divine order vs human ambition developed?

act3.title: Act III — Corruption of power
scenes: Scene 1 (Banquo's suspicion; murder planned): What suspicions does Banquo voice? How does power corrupt Macbeth here?
Scene 2 (Macbeths grow apart): How has their dynamic shifted? Could Macbeth now be more ruthless than Lady Macbeth?
Scene 3 (Murder of Banquo): Why is Fleance's escape symbolically important?
Scene 4 (Banquet and Banquo's ghost): What does Macbeth's reaction reveal about his guilt? How does the banquet mirror political disintegration?
Scene 5 (Hecate's plan): How does this scene develop ideas of fate, free will, and illusion?
Scene 6 (Lennox discusses unrest): How is tyranny contrasted with rightful kingship?

act4.title: Act IV — False security
scenes: Scene 1 (Apparitions): How does Shakespeare use equivocation? How is false security central to Macbeth's tragedy?
Scene 2 (Murder of Lady Macduff): How does focusing on innocence and family affect the audience?
Scene 3 (Malcolm and Macduff plan rebellion): Why does Malcolm test Macduff? How is rightful leadership constructed?

act5.title: Act V — Downfall
scenes: Scene 1 (Lady Macbeth sleepwalking): What does her unconscious speech reveal? How is the blood/washing motif used? Madness as justice or tragedy?
Scene 2 (Scottish forces gather): How is Macbeth's isolation suggested before battle?
Scene 3 (Macbeth clings to prophecies): How does denial and bravado show in his language?
Scene 4 (Birnam Wood marches): How is dramatic irony used? Is fate working with or against human agency?
Scene 5 ('Tomorrow' soliloquy): How does this reflect existential despair? Could Macbeth be sympathetic in his final moments?
Scene 6–8 (Final battle and death): How does Macbeth face death? Could his final fight partially redeem him?

keyQuotes.title: Key quotes (memorise these)
items: 'Fair is foul, and foul is fair' (1.1, Witches) — establishes theme of appearance vs reality.
'I dare do all that may become a man; / Who dares do more is none' (1.7, Macbeth) — conscience speaking before he's overruled.
'Look like the innocent flower, / But be the serpent under't' (1.5, Lady Macbeth) — deception advice.
'Is this a dagger which I see before me' (2.1, Macbeth) — hallucination before murder.
'Will all great Neptune's ocean wash this blood / Clean from my hand?' (2.2, Macbeth) — overwhelming guilt.
'We have scorched the snake, not killed it' (3.2, Macbeth) — fear that threats remain.
'Out, damned spot! Out, I say!' (5.1, Lady Macbeth) — guilt manifesting in sleepwalking.
'Tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow, / Creeps in this petty pace from day to day' (5.5, Macbeth) — nihilistic despair.

examFormatPage.title: Exam Format — Q1a to Q1e
examFormatPage.intro: The teacher's booklet breaks down exactly how the questions work. You'll get TWO texts: usually a written Macbeth extract + a video clip from the Coen Brothers' Macbeth (Denzel Washington).

structure.title: Question structure
items: Q1a — describe or identify something simple (2 marks). Just give the fact.
Q1b — explain meaning of a phrase / device (3 marks). Use 'because' + technique name.
Q1c — comment on how a technique is created (2 marks). One technique + one effect.
Q1d — analyse the effect of a specific phrase (3 marks). Quote + technique + connotation + effect.
Q1e — compare and contrast both texts (20 marks). Full essay. Criterion A (Analysis) /10 + Criterion B (Organising) /10.

q1eStructure.title: How to structure Q1e — the comparison essay
q1eStructure.body: Introduction: name both texts, briefly state the shared theme/question. Body para 1 — a similarity (both texts show…); analyse technique in text 1 (quote + device + effect); then comparable technique in text 2. Body para 2 — a difference (while text 1 does X, text 2 does Y); analyse both. Body para 3 (optional) — deeper layer of comparison. Conclusion — what does the comparison reveal? Word count target: clear, organised, ~400–500 words. Critical: COMPARE in every paragraph, don't write about one text then the other separately.

markScheme.title: Q1e mark scheme — what the bands look like
items: 9–10 (A): Deep insight, precise terminology, subtle/complex comparisons.
6–8 (A): Clear understanding, some terminology, clear comparison.
3–5 (A): General explanations, similarities/differences too general, examples not fully explained.
1–2 (A): Obvious points ('both show power'), little analysis.
9–10 (B): Comparison threads through every paragraph; logical flow.
6–8 (B): Compare in each paragraph; structure clear.
3–5 (B): Attempts comparison but may treat texts separately.
1–2 (B): Writes about texts one after the other, or uses bullet points.

selfCheck.title: Self-check prompts
items: Did I quote from BOTH texts?
Did I name techniques (metaphor, lighting, sound, structure) accurately?
Did I explain the EFFECT of each technique, not just spot it?
Did I compare in every paragraph (not text-by-text)?
Did I write under timed conditions to build exam stamina?

progressTestPage.title: Progress Test — Vocabulary & SPaG
progressTestPage.intro: From the Progress Test Revision Booklet. Vocabulary banks for analytical writing, SPaG rules, British vs American spelling.

survivalVocab.title: Survival / cold setting vocabulary
survivalVocab.body: Glacial, Inhospitable, Barren, Crystalline, Desolate, Relentless, Fragile, Endure, Eerie, Bleak, Solitude, Resilient, Ritual, Burden, Adapt, Thrive.

items.positive: POSITIVE: Jubilant, Tranquil, Uplifting, Serene, Hopeful, Joyful, Whimsical, Light-hearted, Celebratory, Enchanting, Comforting.
items.neutral: NEUTRAL: Reflective, Meditative, Ambiguous, Mysterious, Solemn, Thoughtful, Nostalgic, Clinical, Detached, Philosophical, Measured, Serious.
items.negative: NEGATIVE: Oppressive, Melancholic, Foreboding, Bleak, Dismal, Hostile, Anxious, Isolated, Harrowing, Desolate.

items.supportive: SUPPORTIVE: Admiring, Affirming, Endorsing, Celebratory, Sympathetic, Encouraging, Empathetic, Respectful, Optimistic.
items.critical: CRITICAL: Condemning, Dismissive, Disparaging, Disapproving, Concerned, Sceptical, Alarmed, Cautionary, Cynical, Mocking, Critical.
items.balanced: BALANCED: Neutral, Objective, Measured, Analytical, Tentative, Contemplative, Evaluative, Inquisitive, Informative, Reserved, Considered.

synthesis.title: Synthesis sentence starters
items: Both texts show that…
While one writer believes…, the other…
A shared idea in both extracts is…
Text A highlights ____, whereas Text B emphasises ____.
Together, the texts suggest that…

shadesOfMeaning.title: Shades of meaning — choose your word carefully
items: Barren vs Desolate — barren is lifeless physical; desolate is emotional emptiness.
Endure vs Tolerate — endure implies strength through real hardship; tolerate is mild annoyance.
Relentless vs Determined — relentless is negative/aggressive; determined is positive willpower.

spag.title: SPaG essentials
items: It's = it is. Its = possession. Pronouns (yours, its, theirs) NEVER take an apostrophe.
Run-on sentences — fix with full stop, semicolon, or conjunction.
their / there / they're — possession / place / they are.
your / you're — possession / you are.
'Should of' is NEVER correct. Always 'should have' (could have, might have).
Apostrophes for possession: Luka's book (one Luka); the students' books (multiple students).

spelling.title: British vs American spelling
items: −our / −or: colour/color, honour/honor, behaviour/behavior, favour/favor, neighbour/neighbor.
−re / −er: centre/center, theatre/theater, metre/meter, litre/liter, calibre/caliber.
−ise / −ize: organise/organize, realise/realize, recognise/recognize, analyse/analyze.
Double consonants: travelling/traveling, labelled/labeled, cancelled/canceled, marvellous/marvelous.
Misc: aeroplane/airplane, maths/math, programme/program, grey/gray, cheque/check.
Use BRITISH spelling in IB MYP exams.

devicesExamples.title: Literary devices with worked examples
items: Personification — 'The wind whispered through the trees.'
Zoomorphism — describing humans as animals: 'She fought like a tiger.'
Repetition — emphasises emotion: 'I will not, I will not, I will not give up.'
Metaphor — 'Time is a thief.'
Alliteration — 'wild and windy weather.'
Hyperbole — deliberate exaggeration: 'I've told you a million times.'
Sensory imagery — appeals to five senses.
Simile — comparison with like/as: 'as cold as ice.'

extras.title: English — Extras
extras.intro: Topics that go deeper than the G9 revision list — critical thinking, classic literature, advanced vocabulary, exam-ready quotes.

criticalThinking.title: Critical thinking
items: Identify the claim — what is the author arguing?
Look for evidence — facts, statistics, expert opinion, anecdotes.
Spot fallacies — straw man, false dichotomy, ad hominem, appeal to authority.
Detect bias — emotional language, selective evidence, loaded questions.
Evaluate sources — peer-reviewed > newspaper > blog > social media.

shakespeare.title: Shakespeare — quick intro
shakespeare.body: William Shakespeare (1564–1616) wrote 39 plays and 154 sonnets. Key plays: Romeo and Juliet (tragedy), Macbeth (ambition), Hamlet (revenge, doubt), A Midsummer Night's Dream (comedy). Iambic pentameter — 10 syllables per line, alternating unstressed/stressed. Famous quote: 'To be, or not to be: that is the question.' Themes resonate today: love, power, identity, mortality.

vocab.title: Advanced vocabulary for essays
items: Ubiquitous — present everywhere (use instead of 'common').
Profound — having deep insight or impact.
Articulate (v) — express clearly; (adj) — well-spoken.
Paradox — apparent contradiction that reveals truth.
Catalyst — agent that causes change.
Pivotal — critically important.
Inevitable — certain to happen.
Furthermore / Moreover — and on top of that.
Notwithstanding — despite this.
Consequently / Therefore — as a result.

essayQuotes.title: Quotes to use in essays
items: On change: 'The only constant is change.' — Heraclitus
On knowledge: 'I think, therefore I am.' — Descartes
On society: 'It takes a village to raise a child.' — African proverb
On nature: 'In every walk with nature one receives far more than he seeks.' — John Muir
On power: 'With great power comes great responsibility.' — Voltaire (and Spider-Man)
On time: 'Tomorrow is the most important thing in life.' — John Wayne

poetry.title: Analysing poetry
poetry.body: 1) Read aloud at least twice. 2) Form: lines, stanzas, rhyme scheme (ABAB? AABB?). 3) Meter: count stressed syllables (iambic? trochaic?). 4) Imagery: sensory details, symbols. 5) Tone: melancholic, jubilant, ironic? 6) Theme: what big idea is explored? 7) Speaker: who is saying this and to whom? Pull short quotes when analysing — and always link back to the question.

textTypesPage.title: Text Types (Criterion C)
textTypesPage.intro: Nine text types you may be asked to write. Match the format and audience carefully.

essay.name: Essay
essay.rule: Formal, structured argument with introduction → thesis → body paragraphs → conclusion. Each body paragraph follows PEEL (Point, Evidence, Explain, Link). Written in third person, formal register. ~800-1200 words at G9.
examples: Introduction: 'Climate change is the defining challenge of our era. This essay argues that immediate action on emissions is essential, drawing on scientific consensus and economic analysis.'
Body opening: 'First, the scientific evidence is unambiguous.'
Conclusion opener: 'In conclusion, while challenges remain, the case for urgent action is overwhelming.'
Avoid: contractions (don't → do not), 'I think' (use 'this essay argues'), slang.
essay.tip: Always answer the question in the introduction (state your thesis), then prove it. Don't list — argue.

letter.name: Letter
letter.rule: Has sender's address (top right), date, recipient's address (left, formal letters), salutation, body, closing, signature. Format depends on formality: formal (Dear Sir/Madam, Yours faithfully) vs informal (Dear John, Best wishes).
examples: Formal opening: 'Dear Mr Smith,' / closing: 'Yours sincerely, [name]' (when you know the name) or 'Yours faithfully' (when you don't).
Informal opening: 'Dear Granny,' / closing: 'Love, Luka' or 'Best wishes,'.
Letter of complaint: state the issue, evidence, action requested, deadline.
Letter to editor: short, punchy, opinion-driven.
letter.watchOut: DON'T mix formal and informal: never write 'Dear Sir/Madam' then 'Take care!' — register must match throughout.

email.name: Email
email.rule: Subject line (concise but informative), greeting (Hi/Dear/Hello), body in short paragraphs, sign-off (Kind regards/Best wishes/Cheers). More direct than a letter. Common in school and work contexts.
examples: Subject: 'Request: extension on history essay deadline'
Body: 'Hi Mrs Williams, I'm writing to ask for a two-day extension on the history essay due Friday. I've been unwell since Monday. Thank you for considering this. Best wishes, Luka.'
Reply: 'Hi Luka, Thanks for letting me know. The new deadline is Monday at 9am. Best, Mrs W.'
email.tip: First sentence states your purpose — readers scan, they don't read line by line. Subject lines decide if your email gets opened.

speech.name: Speech
speech.rule: Written to be SPOKEN. Greet audience, open with a hook (story, question, statistic), structure as 'tell them what you'll say → say it → tell them what you said', call to action at the end. Use rhetorical devices: tricolons, repetition, direct address.
examples: Hook: 'Imagine waking up tomorrow with no internet. How long before you'd cope?'
Tricolon: 'We need education, opportunity, and hope.'
Direct address: 'Friends, fellow students, you have the power to change this.'
Famous example: MLK 'I Have a Dream' — uses anaphora ('I have a dream that...').
Call to action: 'So sign the petition. Speak to your MP. Be the change.'
speech.tip: Read it aloud as you write. Use shorter sentences than essays — listeners can't re-read. Pause cues: ellipses, dashes.

newspaper.name: Newspaper Article
newspaper.rule: Headline (catchy, 5-10 words), byline (writer's name + date), lead paragraph answering 5 W's (Who, What, Where, When, Why) + How, then inverted pyramid: most important info first, details lower down. Third person, past tense, formal but accessible.
examples: Headline: 'Local school wins national robotics prize'
Lead: 'NLCS Dubai students Sarah Khan and Omar Patel claimed the £5,000 top prize at the UK Robotics Challenge in London on Saturday, beating 200 other schools.'
Quote from interviewee: 'It was incredible,' said Khan, 15. 'We worked on the code for six months.'
Avoid: opinions in news articles (those go in editorials/columns).
newspaper.tip: Inverted pyramid — if your editor cuts the last paragraph, the story should still make sense. Crucial info goes UP TOP.

online.name: Online Article
online.rule: Title with SEO-friendly keywords, short paragraphs (2-3 sentences max), subheadings every 200-300 words, hyperlinks, optional images. Written for skimming. Conversational but informative tone.
examples: Title: '5 reasons your school WiFi is so slow (and how to fix it)'
Subheading: '1. Outdated routers'
Paragraph: 'Most school routers are over 5 years old. They simply weren't built for 500 students streaming simultaneously.'
Sites: BBC News, The Guardian, BuzzFeed (vastly different registers but all 'online').
online.tip: First 3 sentences must HOOK and PROMISE value, or readers leave. Most online readers don't finish the article.

blog.name: Blog Article
blog.rule: Conversational, personal voice in FIRST PERSON. Title invites curiosity. Open with a personal anecdote or question. Use 'I', 'you', contractions. Often ends with an open question to spark comments.
examples: Title: 'Why I quit Netflix for a month (and what I learned)'
Opening: 'So three weeks ago, I deleted Netflix from my phone. I know — radical, right?'
Body: 'Here's the thing nobody told me about screen-time addiction...'
Closing: 'What's the one app you couldn't live without? Drop a comment below.'
Top blogs: Wait But Why, Mark Manson, The Pudding.
blog.tip: Authenticity > polish. Readers want a real voice, not a textbook. Mistakes can be charming if intentional.

guide.name: Revision Guide
guide.rule: Clear headings + subheadings, bulleted lists, definitions in bold or boxes, worked examples. Designed for SCANNING and RECALL. No personal voice. Educational tone.
examples: Heading: 'Quadratic equations'
Definition box: 'A quadratic equation is one in the form ax² + bx + c = 0, where a ≠ 0.'
Worked example: 'Solve x² - 5x + 6 = 0. Factorise: (x-2)(x-3) = 0. So x = 2 or x = 3.'
Tip box: '💡 Always check by substituting back into the original equation.'
This very page is a revision guide.
guide.tip: Layout matters as much as content — a well-formatted guide with whitespace and clear hierarchy is more useful than a wall of text.

script.name: Script
script.rule: Scene heading (INT./EXT., LOCATION, TIME), character names in CAPITALS before their lines, dialogue, stage directions in italics or brackets. Action and emotion shown, not told.
examples: Scene heading: 'INT. SCHOOL CAFETERIA — DAY'
Action: 'LUKA sits alone, picking at his food. SARA enters, sees him.'
Dialogue: 'SARA: Mind if I sit?'
Parenthetical: '(quietly) Yeah... whatever.'
Plays use slightly different format (e.g., character names centered) vs screenplays (Hollywood format).
script.tip: Show, don't tell. Don't write 'Luka is sad' — write 'Luka stares at his lunch without eating.' Dialogue should sound like real people speaking, not perfect sentences.

devicesPage.title: Literary Devices
devicesPage.intro: Identify devices when reading; use them deliberately when writing.

metaphor.name: Metaphor
metaphor.rule: Direct comparison stating that one thing IS another, without 'like'/'as'. Powerful because it implies identity, not just similarity. Often extends across sentences or paragraphs (extended metaphor).
examples: 'Time is a thief' — time steals our hours like a thief steals possessions.
'The classroom was a zoo' — chaos and noise compared without comparing.
Shakespeare in Macbeth: 'Life's but a walking shadow' — extended metaphor of life as a fleeting actor.
Sport: 'He's a beast on the field' — animal qualities applied to a player.
metaphor.tip: When analysing: identify the comparison + explain the CONNOTATIONS (what feelings or ideas does the metaphor carry?).

simile.name: Simile
simile.rule: Explicit comparison using 'like' or 'as'. Less forceful than metaphor (acknowledges difference), but vivid and accessible. Common in everyday speech.
examples: 'She fought like a tiger' — ferocity compared explicitly.
'As cold as ice' — temperature/emotion comparison.
Robert Burns: 'O my Luve's like a red, red rose' — beauty + fragility.
'His voice was as smooth as silk' — sensory comparison.
'Like trying to nail jelly to a wall' — futility.
simile.tip: Cliché alert: 'as cold as ice', 'as old as the hills' — examiners want FRESH similes. Create your own to score higher.

anaphora.name: Anaphora
anaphora.rule: Repetition of word(s) at the START of successive clauses/sentences. Creates rhythm, emphasis, and emotional build-up. Classic rhetorical device in speeches.
examples: Churchill: 'We shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds, we shall fight in the fields...'
MLK: 'I have a dream that one day... I have a dream that one day... I have a dream today.'
Shakespeare: 'Tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow / Creeps in this petty pace...'
Children's chant: 'I want it now. I want it here. I want it free.'
anaphora.tip: Anaphora is everywhere in political speeches. When analysing: count the repetitions and discuss what they emphasise.

alliteration.name: Alliteration
alliteration.rule: Repetition of the same INITIAL CONSONANT sound across nearby words. Audible pattern that catches the ear; can sound musical, harsh, or comedic depending on the sound.
examples: 'Wild and windy weather' — light, energetic feel from /w/ sounds.
'Slimy, slithering snake' — sibilance creates menace.
Headlines: 'Pelosi promises Putin pushback' — memorable through alliteration.
Tongue twister: 'Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers'.
Brand names: Coca-Cola, Dunkin' Donuts, Mickey Mouse — alliterative for memorability.
alliteration.tip: Different consonants give different effects: /s/ = sibilance (snake-like, calm or sinister), /b/ /p/ = plosives (force, aggression), /l/ = liquid (smooth, gentle), /m/ = soft, comforting.

personification.name: Personification
personification.rule: Giving HUMAN traits, emotions, or actions to non-human things (objects, animals, nature, abstract concepts). Makes descriptions vivid and creates emotional resonance.
examples: 'The wind whispered through the trees' — wind given the human action of whispering.
'Opportunity knocks' — chance acting like a visitor at a door.
'The flowers danced in the breeze' — plants given human movement.
'The shadows crept across the floor' — eerie effect.
'Justice is blind' — abstract concept given a physical quality.
personification.tip: Look for unusual verbs paired with non-human subjects. 'Smiled', 'wept', 'screamed' applied to weather, buildings, or feelings → personification.

hyperbole.name: Hyperbole
hyperbole.rule: DELIBERATE exaggeration for emphasis or comic effect. Not meant to be taken literally. Common in spoken English and advertising. Greek for 'overshooting'.
examples: 'I've told you a million times' — frustration, not maths.
'I could eat a horse' — extreme hunger.
'This bag weighs a ton' — heavy, but obviously not 1000 kg.
'It's been forever' — emotional emphasis on waiting.
Macbeth: 'Will all great Neptune's ocean wash this blood clean?' — hyperbole highlighting guilt.
hyperbole.tip: Hyperbole reveals EMOTIONAL TRUTH even though factually false. Analyse: what feeling is being exaggerated?

rhetoricalQuestion.name: Rhetorical question
rhetoricalQuestion.rule: A question asked for EFFECT, not for an answer. Engages the listener/reader, prompts thought, or makes a statement persuasively. Often answer is obvious or implied.
examples: 'Who could resist such a deal?' — the answer 'nobody' is implied.
'Is this the kind of country we want to live in?' — political speeches.
Shakespeare: 'What's in a name? That which we call a rose / By any other name would smell as sweet' (Romeo & Juliet).
Advertising: 'Why pay more?' — pushes you toward the cheap option.
MLK: 'When will you be satisfied?' — challenges audience.
rhetoricalQuestion.tip: When writing argument essays, use 1-2 rhetorical questions for impact. Too many = annoying.

imagery.name: Imagery
imagery.rule: Vivid SENSORY description appealing to sight, sound, taste, touch, or smell. Helps the reader experience the scene. Often combined with other devices.
examples: 'Crisp autumn leaves crunched underfoot' — sound + touch + visual.
'The room smelled of lavender and old books' — smell.
'A bitter, metallic taste filled her mouth' — taste + texture.
'Slanting golden light spilled across the floor' — visual + movement.
Shakespeare: 'It is the east, and Juliet is the sun' — visual + metaphor.
imagery.tip: Five senses: identify which sense(s) the imagery appeals to. Multi-sensory imagery is most powerful.

onomatopoeia.name: Onomatopoeia
onomatopoeia.rule: Word IMITATES a sound — the spelling/pronunciation matches what it describes. Adds aural quality to writing. Common in poetry, comics, children's books.
examples: 'The bees buzzed; the door creaked' — audio mimicry.
'Splash, fizz, pop, crackle' — varied sound effects.
Children's books: 'Moo, Quack, Baa' (animal sounds).
Comics: 'POW! BANG! THWACK!' (action sounds).
Poetry: 'The murmuring of innumerable bees' — Tennyson, using soft sounds to mimic the buzzing.
onomatopoeia.tip: Different languages have different onomatopoeia (English: bark = 'woof'; Russian: 'gav'; Japanese: 'wan'). Cultural!

irony.name: Irony
irony.rule: Three types: (1) VERBAL — saying one thing, meaning opposite (sarcasm); (2) SITUATIONAL — outcome opposite to expectation; (3) DRAMATIC — audience knows something characters don't.
examples: Verbal: 'Oh, lovely weather' said during a downpour.
Situational: A traffic warden's car gets a parking ticket. A fire station burning down.
Dramatic: In Romeo and Juliet, the audience knows Juliet is alive when Romeo finds her — but Romeo doesn't.
Macbeth dramatic irony: Duncan calls Macbeth's castle 'pleasant' — the audience knows he's about to be murdered there.
Literary irony: Oedipus searches for the murderer, not knowing it's himself.
irony.tip: Don't confuse irony with coincidence. True irony has POETIC JUSTICE or pointed contrast — not just any unexpected thing.

symbolism.name: Symbolism
symbolism.rule: An object, character, colour, or action that REPRESENTS something abstract beyond its literal meaning. Universal vs cultural symbols (a dove for peace is widely understood; a chrysanthemum varies by country).
examples: 'A dove symbolises peace' — universal.
Macbeth: blood symbolises guilt ('a little water clears us of this deed').
The colour red — love, danger, revolution, anger (context-dependent).
Storm in literature = chaos, conflict, emotional upheaval.
The Great Gatsby — green light = unreachable dreams, hope.
Crown = power, kingship, responsibility.
symbolism.tip: Look for objects mentioned MULTIPLE TIMES in a text — they're often symbolic. Ask 'what abstract idea is this connected to?'

foreshadowing.name: Foreshadowing
foreshadowing.rule: Hint or clue about what will happen LATER in the text. Creates suspense, builds tension, and can make endings feel inevitable. Often subtle on first read.
examples: 'Dark clouds gathered as the heroes set off' — bad weather hints at trouble ahead.
Romeo: 'I fear, too early; for my mind misgives / Some consequence yet hanging in the stars / Shall bitterly begin his fearful date...' — Romeo himself foreshadows their doom.
Witches' prophecies in Macbeth — foreshadow his rise AND fall.
A character mentions they 'can't swim' early in a story — likely water danger later.
Title is often foreshadowing: '1984', 'The Catcher in the Rye'.
foreshadowing.tip: Reread the opening AFTER finishing — you'll spot foreshadowing you missed. Great writers plant clues invisibly.

grammarPage.title: Grammar Essentials
grammarPage.intro: Frequent grammar pitfalls and the rules that fix them.

present.name: Present simple
present.use: Habits, repeated actions, general facts, timetables, instructions.
present.form: I / you / we / they + verb. He / she / it + verb + s. Negative: do / does + not + verb. Question: Do / Does + subject + verb?
examples: She writes essays every weekend.
Water boils at 100 °C.
The train leaves at 7 a.m.
He doesn't eat meat.
present.tip: Don't forget the −s on third person singular: 'He goes' (not 'He go'). For verbs ending in −y after consonant: study → studies.

presentCont.name: Present continuous (progressive)
presentCont.use: Actions happening RIGHT NOW, or temporary actions, or future arrangements.
presentCont.form: Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing. Negative: am/is/are + not + verb-ing. Question: Am/Is/Are + subject + verb-ing?
examples: She is writing an essay (right now).
I'm staying with my aunt this week (temporary).
We're meeting Tom at 6 (future arrangement).
presentCont.watchOut: Stative verbs (know, believe, love, want, see) usually stay in present simple: 'I know him' — NOT 'I am knowing him'.

past.name: Past simple
past.use: Completed actions at a specific time in the past. Sequence of past events. Past states.
past.form: Regular: verb + ed (worked, played). Irregular: separate forms (went, saw, wrote). Negative: did + not + base verb. Question: Did + subject + base verb?
examples: She wrote three essays yesterday.
We went to Paris last summer.
He didn't see the film.
Did you eat lunch?
past.tip: Time markers: yesterday, last (week / year), ago, in 2020, when I was younger.

pastCont.name: Past continuous
pastCont.use: Action in progress at a past moment, often interrupted by another action. Background scene-setting.
examples: She was writing when the lights went out.
While we were having dinner, the phone rang.
It was raining all morning.
pastCont.tip: Pair past continuous (long, background action) with past simple (short, interrupting action): 'I was reading when he called.'

presentPerfect.name: Present perfect
presentPerfect.use: Past action with present relevance. Life experience (no specific time). Action that started in the past and continues now. Recent finished action.
presentPerfect.form: Subject + have/has + past participle. Negative: have/has + not + pp. Question: Have/Has + subject + pp?
examples: She has written three essays (this year — still ongoing).
I've never been to Japan (life experience).
We've lived here since 2010 (started past, continues).
He's just finished his homework (recent).
presentPerfect.watchOut: Use present perfect with 'just', 'already', 'yet', 'ever', 'never', 'since', 'for'. Don't use it with a specific finished time ('yesterday' takes past simple, not present perfect).

future.name: Future — 'will' vs 'going to' vs present continuous
future.use: 'will' — decision made AT the moment of speaking; predictions; promises. 'going to' — plans and intentions made BEFORE speaking; predictions based on evidence. Present continuous — fixed arrangements with another person.
future.form: Will: subject + will + base verb. Going to: subject + am/is/are + going to + base verb.
examples: 'I'll help you with that.' (just decided).
'It will rain tomorrow.' (prediction).
'I'm going to study medicine.' (plan).
'Look at those clouds — it's going to rain.' (evidence-based).
'I'm meeting Sarah at 4.' (arrangement).
future.tip: For exam writing, mix tenses naturally. Don't overuse 'will' for everything — use 'going to' for plans, present continuous for arrangements.

comma.name: Comma (,)
comma.use: Separates items in a list; marks pauses; separates clauses; goes after introductory phrases.
examples: I bought bread, cheese, and milk. (list)
After the storm, the village was flooded. (introductory phrase)
Macbeth, who was once loyal, becomes a tyrant. (non-defining clause)
The film was long, but I enjoyed it. (before coordinating conjunction)
comma.watchOut: Don't use a comma to join two complete sentences (this is a 'comma splice'): WRONG — 'I went out, it was cold.' Use a full stop, semicolon, or conjunction.

semicolon.name: Semicolon (;)
semicolon.use: Joins two closely related independent clauses (each could stand alone). Also separates items in a complex list where items contain commas.
examples: Macbeth kills Duncan; immediately he is haunted by guilt.
She didn't speak; she just walked away.
We visited Cairo, Egypt; Rome, Italy; and Athens, Greece.
semicolon.tip: If you could replace the semicolon with a full stop without losing meaning, you've used it correctly.

colon.name: Colon (:)
colon.use: Introduces a list, an example, an explanation, or a quotation.
examples: She had three goals: ambition, power, and survival.
Macbeth makes one fatal mistake: he listens to the witches.
Lady Macbeth declares: 'Unsex me here.'
colon.watchOut: What comes BEFORE the colon should be a complete sentence. Don't use a colon after 'such as' or 'for example'.

apostrophe.name: Apostrophe (')
apostrophe.use: Shows possession OR shows that letters are missing (contraction).
examples: Luka's book — book belongs to Luka (singular owner).
The students' essays — essays of the students (plural owners).
Don't = do not. It's = it is. They're = they are.
James's coat / James' coat — both accepted; pick one and be consistent.
apostrophe.watchOut: Pronouns (its, yours, hers, ours, theirs) NEVER take an apostrophe. 'Its' = possession; 'it's' = it is.

dash.name: Dash (—)
dash.use: Marks a sudden break, an aside, an emphasis, or replaces parentheses for a more dramatic feel.
examples: Macbeth — once loyal, now a tyrant — falls in the final battle.
She wanted only one thing — power.
He paused — then drew the dagger.
dash.tip: Em dash (—) is longer than a hyphen (-). Use it sparingly; overuse makes prose feel breathless.

agreement.title: Subject–verb agreement
agreement.body: Singular subject takes a singular verb; plural subject takes a plural verb. Sounds simple — but tricky cases trip up most students.
rules: Singular: 'The boy runs.' Plural: 'The boys run.'
Words BETWEEN subject and verb don't change agreement: 'The list of items IS on the table' (list is, not items are).
Either / or, neither / nor — verb agrees with the closer subject: 'Neither the captain nor the sailors ARE on board.' 'Neither the sailors nor the captain IS on board.'
Collective nouns (team, family, government) can be singular or plural depending on emphasis on the group as one unit or individuals: 'The team is winning' vs 'The team are arguing among themselves.'
Indefinite pronouns 'each', 'every', 'everyone', 'nobody', 'somebody' are SINGULAR: 'Everybody HAS arrived.'
'A number of' is plural, 'the number of' is singular: 'A number of students are absent.' 'The number of students is rising.'

yourYoure.name: your vs you're
examples: Is this your book? (possessive)
You're late again. (you are)
yourYoure.tip: If you can replace it with 'you are', use 'you're'. Otherwise it's 'your'.

itsIts.name: its vs it's
itsIts.rule: 'its' = belonging to it (possessive — NO apostrophe). 'it's' = it is or it has (contraction).
examples: The dog wagged its tail. (possessive)
It's raining. (it is)
It's been a long day. (it has)
itsIts.tip: If you can replace it with 'it is' or 'it has', use 'it's'. Possessive 'its' never has an apostrophe — pronouns don't.

thereTheirTheyre.name: there / their / they're
thereTheirTheyre.rule: 'there' = a place or 'there is/are'. 'their' = belonging to them. 'they're' = they are.
examples: Put it over there. (place)
There are five students. (there are)
Their essays were excellent. (belonging to them)
They're studying for the exam. (they are)
thereTheirTheyre.tip: Hear (a place) → 'there' has 'here' in it. 'Their' starts with 'thei' like 'theirs'. 'They're' = they are.

lessFewer.name: less vs fewer
lessFewer.rule: 'fewer' for things you can COUNT (cars, people, mistakes). 'less' for things you CAN'T count (time, money, water).
examples: Fewer cars on the road this morning.
We have less time than we thought.
I made fewer mistakes than last week.
lessFewer.tip: Test: can you put a number in front? 'Five mistakes' → fewer. 'Five times' → less (because time is uncountable, even though 'times' is countable in this phrase).

affectEffect.name: affect vs effect
affectEffect.rule: 'affect' = VERB, meaning to influence. 'effect' = NOUN, meaning the result.
examples: The decision will affect everyone. (verb)
The effect of the decision was huge. (noun)
Climate change affects ecosystems. (verb)
One effect of climate change is sea level rise. (noun)
affectEffect.tip: Stormwarning: 'effect' CAN be a verb meaning 'to bring about' ('to effect change'), but at G9 stick to the verb/noun rule.

thanThen.name: than vs then
thanThen.rule: 'than' = COMPARISON. 'then' = TIME or sequence ('next, after that').
examples: She is taller than her brother. (comparison)
First we ate, then we walked home. (sequence)
If it rains, then we'll stay in. (consequence)
thanThen.tip: Comparison → think 'compare', both have an A. Time → then has 'en' like 'when'.

readingPage.title: Reading Skills
readingPage.intro: How to break down unseen texts in exam conditions.

skim.name: Skim
skim.use: Get the gist of an unseen text in 60 seconds — before any analysis.
skim.rule: Read the title, first paragraph, last paragraph, and the first sentence of every other paragraph. Note: what's the text about? Who wrote it? What tone?
skim.tip: Don't read every word. Your goal is to know WHAT and ROUGHLY HOW, not the detail.

scan.name: Scan
scan.use: Find a specific piece of information (a name, date, key word, quote).
scan.rule: Move your eyes quickly down the page looking only for the keyword. Stop when you find it; then read the sentence around it.
examples: Q: 'When was Macbeth crowned?' → scan for years or king-related words.
Q: 'Find a metaphor in paragraph 2.' → scan paragraph 2 for figurative language.

annotate.name: Annotate
annotate.use: Mark up the text as you read so you can find evidence quickly when answering.
annotate.rule: Underline key phrases. Circle techniques (metaphor, alliteration). In the margin, write tone words (foreboding, jubilant). Note the SHIFT — where does the tone change?
annotate.tip: Use a system: device = circle, theme = underline, mood word = box. Stay consistent.

infer.name: Infer
infer.use: Read between the lines — what is the author IMPLYING but not directly saying?
infer.rule: Combine clues in the text + your own world knowledge. Ask: 'What does this detail TELL me about the character / situation / writer's view?'
examples: 'She slammed the door' → not just describing an action; implies anger.
'The sky was the colour of bruises' → not just weather; implies pain or sadness.

literal.name: Literal questions
literal.use: Test direct understanding: 'What does the text say?' Lowest level.
literal.rule: Find the evidence directly in the text. Don't interpret. Quote or paraphrase exactly. Usually 1–2 marks.
examples: 'State one reason Macbeth feels afraid.' → quote directly.
'Name two effects of climate change mentioned.' → list directly from text.

inferential.name: Inferential questions
inferential.use: Test reading between the lines: 'What does this suggest?'
inferential.rule: Combine textual clues with your own reasoning. Use phrases like 'this suggests…', 'implies…', 'hints at…'. Always link back to a specific word or detail.
examples: 'What does the writer's choice of "cold" suggest about the relationship?' → suggests emotional distance, not physical temperature.

language.name: Language analysis
language.use: Test how the writer creates meaning: 'How does the writer use language?'
language.rule: QUOTE the language → NAME the technique (metaphor, sibilance, asyndeton) → EXPLAIN the effect on the reader. The 'effect' part is what earns marks.
examples: The metaphor 'a sea of doubt' likens hesitation to water — suggesting Macbeth is drowning in his choices, overwhelmed and unable to act.
language.tip: Never say 'the writer uses a metaphor to make it more interesting.' Always say WHAT specific feeling, image, or idea the technique creates.

evaluation.name: Evaluation questions
evaluation.use: Test critical judgement: 'How effective is X?' / 'To what extent…?'
evaluation.rule: Take a clear position (very effective / partly effective / ineffective) and DEFEND it with multiple pieces of evidence. Consider counter-arguments.
examples: 'How effective is the writer's use of imagery?' → Argue: highly effective because it creates X, Y, Z; though one might say Z is overused, this is offset by W.

PEEL.title: PEEL paragraph structure
PEEL.body: PEEL is the gold standard for analytical paragraphs. It guarantees you cover every step the examiner is looking for. Use it for EVERY body paragraph in your literature or non-fiction analysis.

steps.p: POINT — open with your topic sentence. State the specific claim this paragraph proves. e.g., 'Shakespeare presents Lady Macbeth as more ruthless than her husband in Act 1.'
steps.e1: EVIDENCE — quote DIRECTLY from the text. Use quotation marks. Keep quotes short — 1 line or less. e.g., 'She demands the spirits to "unsex me here."'
steps.e2: EXPLAIN — analyse what the quote shows. Name the technique. Explain the EFFECT on the reader. e.g., 'The imperative "unsex" is a violent rejection of femininity, suggesting she sees compassion as a weakness preventing the murder.'
steps.l: LINK — connect back to the question and forward to the next paragraph. e.g., 'This ruthless ambition contrasts sharply with Macbeth's hesitation in Scene 7, setting up the central tension of the play.'

PEEL.tip: Hit ALL four every time. Examiners look for them as a checklist. Don't write three or four PEEL paragraphs and skip the conclusion — even 30 seconds of conclusion adds marks.

essaysPage.title: Essay Practice
essaysPage.intro: Sample essay prompts in the IB MYP style. Plan with a thesis, three points, and a conclusion.

essayPrompts.prompt: 'Social media does more harm than good.' Discuss.
essayPrompts.tips: Balance both sides. State your position clearly. Use real examples (Instagram, TikTok). Conclude decisively.

essayPrompts.prompt: 'School uniforms should be abolished.' Argue for or against.
essayPrompts.tips: Take one side firmly. Three reasons with evidence. Address counterargument and refute it.

essayPrompts.prompt: Write a story beginning with: 'The lights flickered, and then…'
essayPrompts.tips: Establish setting. Use varied sentence structure. Include sensory detail. End with a twist or resonance.

essayPrompts.prompt: Describe a place that is special to you. Bring it to life through the five senses.
essayPrompts.tips: Sight, sound, smell, touch, taste. Avoid clichés. Use figurative language sparingly.

essayPrompts.prompt: Write a speech persuading your school to take action on climate change.
essayPrompts.tips: Direct address ('We must…'). Rhetorical question. Tripartite list. Call to action at the end.

checklist: Match the text type and audience: formal letter ≠ blog post
Use a clear structure with paragraphs
Show, don't tell — use specific details
Proofread for spelling, punctuation, and tense consistency
